hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'Iesum' — 'Iesus' is nominative, the subject
hints.Vocative: Vocative would be 'Iesu' — 'Iesus' is nominative here

steps.prompt: What does 'plenus' mean and what case does it govern?

hints.'full' — governs the genitive: Classical 'plenus' can take genitive, but in the Vulgate it takes ablative 'Spiritu'
hints.'flat' — governs the dative: 'plenus' means 'full', not 'flat' — and it governs the ablative
hints.'full' — governs the accusative: 'plenus' does not take the accusative — it governs the ablative here

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'Spiritus' — 'Spiritu' with -u ending is ablative
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'Spiritui' — 'Spiritu' is ablative governed by 'plenus'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'spiritus' genitive ends in -ūs
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'spiritus' genitive ends in -ūs
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'spiritus' genitive ends in -ūs

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'Sanctus' — 'Sancto' agrees with 'Spiritu' in the ablative
hints.Dative: 'Sancto' could be dative, but here it modifies 'Spiritu' which is ablative

steps.prompt: What is the implied verb in this phrase?

hints.est (is): 'est' is present — the narrative context implies the imperfect 'erat'
hints.fuit (was, completed): 'fuit' is perfect — the ongoing state 'full of the Spirit' suggests imperfect 'erat'
hints.habuit (had): 'habuit' means 'had' — the implied verb is 'erat' (to be), not 'to have'

translation: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit
